definition: A device consisting of two different metals immersed in an electrolyte, in which chemical energy is converted into electrical energy. The less noble metal acts as the negative electrode and gradually dissolves, while the more noble metal forms the positive electrode.

An ion-conducting liquid, such as a salt solution or dilute acid. The electrolyte allows ions to move and closes the internal circuit of the galvanic cell. Without an electrolyte, no current can flow.

A ranking of metals according to how readily they form ions. The least noble metals (e.g. potassium, sodium) appear on the left; the most noble (e.g. silver, gold) on the right. Hydrogen is included as a reference point.
A metal that reluctantly forms ions and is resistant to chemical attack. Examples: gold, platinum, silver and copper. Noble metals appear on the right side of the electrochemical series.
A metal that relatively easily gives up electrons and forms ions. It dissolves in acids and tends to corrode. Examples: zinc, iron and magnesium.

A process in which electrical energy is supplied to an electrolyte to drive a chemical reaction. The ions in the solution are converted into atoms at the electrodes. Used to produce pure metals and to coat surfaces.
The electrode connected to the positive terminal of the power source during electrolysis. Oxidation occurs at the anode – negative ions (anions) give up their electrons and are transformed.

The electrode connected to the negative terminal of the power source during electrolysis. Reduction occurs at the cathode – positive ions (cations) gain electrons and are transformed into neutral atoms.
A chemical process in which a substance loses electrons. The charge of the substance increases (becomes more positive). Oxidation and reduction always occur simultaneously.
A chemical process in which a substance gains electrons. The charge of the substance decreases (becomes more negative). Reduction and oxidation always occur together in a redox reaction.
The chemical degradation of a metal in a moist environment, such as when iron rusts. Corrosion is accelerated when two different metals are in electrical contact in the presence of an electrolyte.
A block of less noble metal (usually zinc) attached to a structure to protect it from corrosion. The sacrificial anode dissolves instead of the structure and is replaced regularly.
A rechargeable galvanic cell. The reactions can be reversed using an external power source to store energy again. Examples: the lead-acid battery in cars and lithium-ion batteries in mobile phones.
A surface treatment in which iron or steel is coated with a thin layer of zinc to protect against rust. It can be done by hot-dip galvanising or by electrolytic zinc plating.

An electrolytic surface treatment for aluminium in which the object is connected to the anode in an acid bath. A porous aluminium oxide layer forms on the surface, which can be dyed and provides a hard, corrosion-resistant coating.

A reaction in which oxidation and reduction occur simultaneously. The substance that is oxidised (loses electrons) is called the reducing agent, and the substance that is reduced (gains electrons) is called the oxidising agent.
